core_identity.title: The Sum of the Angles in a Triangle
core_identity.author: Gerry Docherty

curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.subtopic: sum of interior angles in a triangle
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Prove and apply that the interior angles of any triangle sum to 180° using parallel-line reasoning, and use the theorem to solve missing-angle problems and check designs for accuracy.

verse_ality.driving_question: Why do the three interior angles of any triangle always add up to 180°?

knowing_world.focus: Investigate and understand why the three interior angles of any triangle always add up to 180° (two right angles), using the parallel line proof and angle facts on a straight line.
key_concepts: Works for acute, right-angled, and obtuse triangles (Euclidean geometry)
activities: Quick check: if two angles are 50° and 60°, calculate the third angle.
Explain why angles 90°, 60°, 40° cannot form a triangle (sum too large).
Sketch a triangle and add a line parallel to one side; label alternate/corresponding angles to show the 180° sum.

human_story.focus: Connect the triangle angle-sum theorem to architecture, symmetry, stability in structures, and modern uses like triangulation in navigation and GPS.

expressive_self.focus: Represent the 180° angle sum across different triangle types using art, story, or physical manipulation to make the invariant relationship memorable.
activities: Draw acute, right, and obtuse triangles and show the angles adding to 180° using colour bands.
Create a short story or animation where a triangle’s angles ‘meet’ at 180°.
Use coloured paper or ribbons to cut/move angles and test the sum physically.

designed_world.focus: Use a web sketchpad to dynamically explore how changing triangle shapes changes individual angles while keeping the total at 180°, and use the tool to recreate the parallel-line proof.
design_challenges: Drag a vertex and observe angles changing while the sum stays 180°.
Make an obtuse triangle and confirm the sum remains 180°; explain why.
Draw a line parallel to one side through the opposite vertex and explore the proof pathway.
Challenge: try to create a triangle with two right angles; explain why it cannot exist.

regulation_supports: Stand in a triangle shape with two classmates (or mimic with arms): change two ‘angles’ and notice the third adjusts to keep the total constant.

pathways.name: Final Task: Prove the Triangle Angle Sum
pathways.focus: Write a short proof (words and diagrams) showing why the interior angles of a triangle always sum to 180°, using a method observed in the sketch or from class.
products: A diagram of a triangle with a parallel line construction (or equivalent proof structure).
A written explanation of why the angles sum to 180° using correct angle facts and labels.
One real-life example where this matters (e.g., architecture, design, checking drawing accuracy).
A short reflection on what surprised you and how the sketchpad supported your thinking.

required: Angle facts on a straight line (180°)
Basic understanding of parallel lines and alternate/corresponding angles
Confidence identifying and labelling angles in triangles

builds_toward: Polygon interior and exterior angle sums
